# Section Structure Templates — Quick Reference

Use this file as a checklist when evaluating each thesis section in `paper-structure-auditor`.
For each section below, tick off the elements that ARE present in the thesis, then flag elements that are missing or misplaced.

---

## Abstract

| # | Required element | Present? |
|---|-----------------|----------|
| 1 | **Sentence 1** — situates reader in the sub-field with an uncontroversially true statement | |
| 2 | **Sentence 2** — establishes a need, unknown quantity, or problem to solve (motivation) | |
| 3 | **Middle sentences** — state the crucial contribution and why it is exciting | |
| 4 | **Jargon defined** — any necessary technical terms are introduced | |
| 5 | **Concrete metric or result** — a number or measurable outcome proving substance | |
| 6 | **Final 1–2 sentences** — implications, broader context, why the paper matters | |

---

## Introduction

| # | Required element | Present? |
|---|-----------------|----------|
| 1 | **Para 1 — Context**: topic introduced, key motivating question stated, why it matters explained | |
| 2 | **Para 2 — Technical background**: what is already known; which established techniques this paper rests on | |
| 3 | **Para 3 — Key contribution**: main claim stated with nuance, detail, and context | |
| 4 | **Para 3.5 — Evidence summary** *(optional but ideal)*: most critical supporting evidence summarised | |
| 5 | **Para 4 — Impact**: reader takeaways, implications, why this is a big deal | |
| 6 | **Contributions list**: bullet-point list at the end — concise claims with brief references to evidence | |

---

## Background

| # | Required element | Present? |
|---|-----------------|----------|
| 1 | All concepts and prior work needed to understand the method are explained | |
| 2 | **Problem Setting subsection**: formal problem definition with notation | |
| 3 | Specialised terms are defined; widely known facts are not over-explained | |
| 4 | Provides sufficient context that a reader unfamiliar with the sub-field can follow the method | |

---

## Methods (Methodology)

| # | Required element | Present? |
|---|-----------------|----------|
| 1 | High-level description of the approach (big picture) | |
| 2 | Fine technical detail (sufficient to reproduce) | |
| 3 | Justification: why each design choice was reasonable and relevant to the claims | |
| 4 | All technical parameters and their implications are specified | |

---

## Results

| # | Required element | Present? |
|---|-----------------|----------|
| 1 | Results are stated clearly — what was measured, what value was obtained | |
| 2 | How to interpret each result is explained | |
| 3 | Comparison against baselines is included | |
| 4 | Experimental setup is described in full reproducible detail | |

---

## Related Work

| # | Required element | Present? |
|---|-----------------|----------|
| 1 | Each cited work is **compared and contrasted** — not just described | |
| 2 | Differences in assumptions or method from this work are explained for each reference | |
| 3 | If a related method applies to this problem: experimental comparison is included **or** reason for exclusion is stated | |
| 4 | Novelty of this work is made explicit relative to the literature | |

---

## Discussion

| # | Required element | Present? |
|---|-----------------|----------|
| 1 | **Limitations** of the work are explained honestly and prominently | |
| 2 | Broader implications and general takeaways are discussed | |
| 3 | Future work directions are outlined | |
| 4 | Contains material that required the full paper's context before it could be stated (not just repeating the intro) | |

---

## Conclusion

| # | Required element | Present? |
|---|-----------------|----------|
| 1 | Main claims and key results are concisely restated | |
| 2 | Contribution to the field is summarised | |
| 3 | Limitations are acknowledged | |
| 4 | Future directions are noted | |

---

## Notes on Ordering and Placement

- Related Work can appear **after** main content (not as the second section) if that aids readability.
- The contributions list in the Introduction should reference specific evidence (figures, tables, sections) — not just list claims in isolation.
- The Problem Setting subsection belongs in **Background**, not in Methods.
